Glossary About
South Asian Traditions

Kundakunda

c. 2nd c. CE

A classical Jain thinker of pure selfhood, perspectival truth, and the contrast between bondage and liberated awareness.

A methodological caution is necessary at the outset. “Kundakunda” is both a revered Digambara ācārya and, in much modern scholarship, a somewhat unstable authorial name attached to a textual tradition. Traditional Jain datings often place him in the early centuries CE, while recent scholarship places him later or treats the Kundakunda corpus as layered. Jayandra Soni notes that Kundakunda’s date remains a “major conundrum,” with recent research often placing him around the fourth or fifth century; the same article reports that Brill’s Encyclopedia of Jainism treats “Kundakunda” as a range of authors active between roughly the third and seventh/eighth centuries. JAINpedia likewise says that nothing reliable is known about his life, that tradition dates him to the second or third century, and that recent scholarship places him around the fourth to fifth centuries. In this report, “Kundakunda” will mean the philosophical position crystallized in the major Kundakunda-attributed Digambara works, especially Samayasāra, Pravacanasāra, Pañcāstikāyasāra, and Niyamasāra, without assuming that every verse is by a single historical hand.

1. Historical position: Kundakunda within Jain and global philosophy of mind

Kundakunda stands at a decisive point in the development of Jain thought. Early Jainism already had a robust ontology of living souls, karma, rebirth, nonviolence, ascetic discipline, and omniscience. The Tattvārthasūtra, associated with Umāsvāti/Umāsvāmin and usually placed between roughly 150 and 400 CE, is often treated as the first systematic Sanskrit synthesis of Jain doctrine. Its famous definition of the self is terse and philosophically loaded: “the defining characteristic of the self is experience,” upayoga. Kundakunda develops this into a more inward, self-reflexive, and soteriological doctrine: the soul is not merely a thing that sometimes has cognition; it is essentially consciousness, knowledge, and pure self-manifestation.

Within Jainism, his distinctive importance is that he radicalizes the distinction between the empirical, karmically conditioned soul and the soul considered from the ultimate or pure standpoint. JAINpedia summarizes this as the distinction between vyavahāra-naya, the conventional standpoint in which one follows right conduct in ordinary practice, and niścaya-naya, the absolute standpoint in which the aspirant knows the real nature of the self. Kundakunda’s works therefore shift Jain philosophy of mind from a mainly taxonomic account of souls, bodies, karmas, and vows toward a theory of inner consciousness, misidentification, and self-realization.

In broader Indian philosophy, Kundakunda belongs to the same world of debates that includes Buddhist no-self theories, Sāṃkhya dualism, Nyāya realism, Vedāntic self-theory, and Jain anekāntavāda or many-sided realism. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes Jain metaphysics as atomist and dualist, centered on self, karmic matter, and the principles of their association; it also notes that Kundakunda’s textual tradition is one of the two major Jain philosophical lineages alongside the Tattvārthasūtra tradition.

In Western terms, Kundakunda enters many of the same problem-spaces that later define philosophy of mind: What is the self? Is consciousness reducible to matter? How does the mind relate to the body? What is the difference between true cognition and error? Is agency mental, bodily, or both? What makes personal identity persist? How does consciousness represent objects? But his answers differ sharply from the dominant Western sequence that runs from Plato and Aristotle through Descartes, Locke, Hume, Kant, Brentano, phenomenology, analytic dualism, and physicalism.

His position can be called a Jain pluralist consciousness-essentialism: there are infinitely many individual conscious substances, each soul’s essence is consciousness or upayoga, consciousness is not produced by the body, and embodied mental life is distorted by a real material karma that obstructs or conditions the soul’s manifestations.

2. Basic metaphysical architecture: jīva and ajīva

Kundakunda’s philosophy of mind begins from Jain metaphysical dualism. Reality is divided fundamentally into jīva, living conscious substance, and ajīva, non-living substance. The non-living includes matter, space, the medium of motion, the medium of rest, and time. Matter, pudgala, includes not only gross bodies but also subtle bodies, karmic particles, speech, breath, and the physical apparatus of mind. The SEP emphasizes this point: in Jainism, matter is the material cause of body, speech, mind, breath, and karma.

This is crucial. Kundakunda does not equate “mind” in the modern psychological sense with an immaterial Cartesian mind. In Jain categories, some of what modern people call “mind” belongs to pudgala: sensory apparatus, bodily cognition, speech, mental instrumentation, karmic obscuration, and affective embodiment. The truly conscious principle is jīva. Therefore, Kundakunda’s philosophy of mind is not simply “mind versus body”; it is consciousness versus non-conscious matter, with the empirical mind-body complex occupying the side of karmically conditioned embodiment.

The Pañcāstikāyasāra identifies the qualities of the soul as consciousness, cetanā, and cognition or upayoga. It further distinguishes pure consciousness from impure consciousness and divides cognition into knowledge-cognition, jñānopayoga, and perception-cognition, darśanopayoga.

The central ontological claim is therefore:

The soul is a conscious substance whose defining manifestation is upayoga; matter may condition, obstruct, or accompany consciousness, but matter is never consciousness.

This places Kundakunda near substance dualism, but not Cartesian dualism. The soul is not a private inner theater housed in a body. Jain souls are embodied across the living universe, are coextensive with bodies while embodied, are affected by karmic matter, and persist through rebirth. Jainism is also not standard panpsychism: not all matter is conscious. Rather, living beings are conscious because jīva is present; matter as such is non-conscious.

3. Upayoga: the core concept of consciousness

The most important term for extracting Kundakunda’s philosophy of mind is upayoga. It is often translated inadequately as “consciousness,” “cognition,” “application,” “attention,” “manifestation of consciousness,” or “conscious activity.” None is perfect. Upayoga is the soul’s active cognitive manifestation: the way consciousness is present, directed, and operative.

The Pañcāstikāyasāra says that the soul is inseparable at all times from two kinds of upayoga: knowledge-cognition and perception-cognition. Its explanatory note defines upayoga as active or attentive consciousness arising with internal and external causes and concomitant with soul-consciousness. It says that through upayoga the soul engages in knowledge or perception of the knowable, and that upayoga distinguishes the soul from the body.

This gives us Kundakunda’s first major philosophy-of-mind thesis:

Consciousness is not an accidental property of the self. It is the self’s defining activity and manifestation.

This differs from views where the self is a bare substrate that happens to possess experiences. In Kundakunda, the soul is not first an inert thing and then secondarily a knower. It is intrinsically knowing. The Pravacanasāra makes this especially explicit: “The one who knows — the soul — is the knowledge”; the soul does not know by means of a separate external quality of knowledge. Knowledge and soul are empirically distinguishable for purposes of speech, but ultimately they are not different.

This is one of the strongest claims in the Kundakundian philosophy of mind. It rejects the model:

self + cognition = conscious subject

and replaces it with:

self = conscious knowing, appearing in varying modes.

4. The self as knowledge: not a possessor of consciousness but consciousness itself

Kundakunda’s position is more radical than merely saying that the soul has consciousness. From the ultimate standpoint, the soul is knowledge. Pravacanasāra explains that the soul is not the knower because of some external quality called knowledge; the soul and knowledge are one, just as fire and heat are empirically distinguishable but actually inseparable.

This matters for several problems in philosophy of mind.

First, it avoids an infinite regress of inner observers. If the soul were an unconscious subject that required knowledge to know, then one might ask: what makes that knowledge known? Kundakunda’s answer is that consciousness is self-manifesting. It does not need a second light to illuminate it.

Second, it gives a reflexive theory of awareness. Pravacanasāra compares the soul to a lamp: the soul illumines itself and others, and therefore is both knowledge and object-of-knowledge. The soul knows itself by its own knowledge.

Third, it treats self-knowledge as metaphysically basic. A soul that fails to know itself is not merely missing a piece of introspective information; it is in a state of spiritual and cognitive alienation.

Fourth, it lets Kundakunda connect epistemology with liberation. Liberation is not primarily relocation to another world or divine reward; it is the full unobstructed manifestation of what consciousness already is.

5. Darśana and jñāna: perception and knowledge

Kundakunda inherits the Jain distinction between darśana and jñāna. Darśana is often translated as perception, intuition, or indeterminate awareness; jñāna is knowledge, cognition, or determinate apprehension. In Pañcāstikāyasāra, knowledge-cognition is described as with details and distinctions, while perception-cognition is without such determinate distinctions. Ordinary, non-omniscient souls experience these successively, while those who have annihilated obstructive karmas experience them simultaneously.

This yields a layered account of cognition:

At the most basic level, consciousness manifests as a non-discursive openness or perceptual awareness. At a more determinate level, consciousness differentiates objects, forms judgments, and knows. At the perfected level, this distinction is overcome in simultaneous omniscient awareness.

This has a loose parallel with Western distinctions between sensation and judgment, acquaintance and description, or pre-reflective and reflective consciousness. But Kundakunda’s account is not merely phenomenological. It is embedded in karmic ontology: cognitive limitation is caused by karmic obstruction, and cognitive perfection requires the destruction of those obstructions.

6. Intentionality: how consciousness knows objects

In contemporary philosophy, intentionality is the mind’s capacity to be about, represent, or stand for things and states of affairs. Brentano made intentionality central to modern philosophy of mind, and the SEP describes intentionality as a major interface between philosophy of mind and philosophy of language.

Kundakunda has a powerful intentionality theory, though not in Brentano’s vocabulary. Pravacanasāra says that the soul is the knower, jñāyaka, and substances are objects of knowledge, jñeya. The knower does not inhere in the known, just as the eye sees objects without entering them. The knower and known do not become one; each retains its nature.

This is a realist theory of intentionality. Objects are not mental constructions generated inside consciousness; they are real substances with their own modes. Yet knowledge can take the form of objects without becoming those objects. Pravacanasāra uses the mirror model: objects are reflected in knowledge, but the knowing soul remains distinct from the foreign objects it knows.

Thus Kundakunda avoids two extremes. He rejects naïve fusion, where knowing an object would require the soul to become the object. He also rejects subjective idealism, where the known object would be nothing but a mental content. His model is: real object, real knower, real cognitive relation, no ontological collapse between knower and known.

7. The mirror and the lamp: two governing metaphors

Two metaphors organize Kundakunda’s philosophy of mind.

The first is the lamp. The soul illumines itself and others. This supports reflexive awareness: consciousness is self-revealing, not known only by inference or by another mental act.

The second is the mirror or crystal. Objects may be reflected in knowledge, and passions or karmic states may appear as though they belong to the soul, but the soul’s pure nature remains distinct. The SEP notes the Kundakundian metaphor of a mirror-like crystal: if a red flower is reflected in a crystal, we see the crystal as red, although the crystal itself is not red. Likewise, attachment and wrong notions are superposed on the self without being its true nature.

Together, these metaphors create a subtle philosophy of appearance. Consciousness is luminous; it manifests objects. But consciousness also misidentifies reflected or associated conditions as its own essence. The core pathology of mind is therefore not lack of information alone; it is misattribution.

8. Error, ignorance, and misidentification

Kundakunda’s theory of error is a theory of self-misidentification. The Samayasāra opens by distinguishing the real self from the self conditioned by karmic matter. The real self rests in right conduct, faith, and knowledge; the empirically conditioned self is associated with karmic material and is “other than the real” from the ultimate standpoint.

This is not a claim that the empirical person is absolutely nonexistent. Rather, it means that the empirical ego is the soul under karmic limitation and false identification. The self mistakenly identifies with body, karmic matter, affective states, actions, social roles, pleasure, pain, and mental dispositions. The commentary preserved in the Samayasāra tradition describes empirical consciousness in cognitive, conative, and affective forms but treats these embodied modifications as consequences of association with material bodies rather than as the pure nature of the self.

Kundakunda therefore anticipates a distinction familiar in later philosophy and psychology: the difference between the empirical ego and a deeper subject of awareness. But his version is metaphysically realist, not merely phenomenological. The pure self is a real substance, and karmic matter is real matter.

9. Karma as a philosophy-of-mind mechanism

In Kundakunda, karma is not merely moral bookkeeping. It is a psychophysical mechanism. Jainism conceives karma as subtle matter that adheres to the soul through passions and obstructs its natural qualities. The SEP lists the four “harming” karmas as delusory karma, knowledge-obstructing karma, perception-obstructing karma, and obstacle karma. These directly concern belief, cognition, perception, and agency.

This means that Jain karma theory is also a theory of mental limitation. Delusion distorts value and self-understanding. Knowledge-obstructing karma limits cognition. Perception-obstructing karma limits immediate awareness. Obstacle karma limits energy or capacity. In modern terms, Kundakunda treats the mind’s defects as simultaneously epistemic, affective, ethical, and material.

The Pañcāstikāyasāra explains the relation between dispositions in the soul and material karma using the Jain distinction between empirical and ultimate standpoints. From the empirical standpoint, material karmas may be said to cause dispositions in the soul; from the ultimate standpoint, material karmas are not the doer of the soul’s dispositions, nor are the soul’s dispositions the doer of material karmas. They are mutually related through instrumental causality, not through identity.

This is a sophisticated response to the mind-body problem. It says: soul and matter are distinct; each transforms in its own domain; but their transformations are coordinated through causal conditions. The soul is the material cause of its own conscious modes. Karma is an instrumental condition for impure conscious modes. Conversely, impure conscious dispositions are instrumental conditions for karmic bondage.

10. Agency: the soul acts only in its own domain

A central Kundakundian thesis is that the self is active only in its own domain. The SEP summarizes Kundakunda’s view: the self is the material cause only of modifications of consciousness, while karmic matter is the indirect instrumental cause of wrong modifications of consciousness; the self is likewise only an indirect instrumental cause of karmic modifications.

This is not passive quietism. Kundakunda is not saying the soul does nothing. He is saying that the soul’s primary activity is conscious transformation: knowing, mis-knowing, attaching, detaching, purifying, realizing. Bodily and karmic processes occur in the domain of matter. The spiritual task is to change the soul’s own modes: from delusion to right insight, from attachment to non-attachment, from impure upayoga to pure upayoga.

This gives a distinctive account of agency. Agency is not primarily the power to rearrange external objects. It is the power of consciousness to transform its own orientation. The deepest action is not bodily motion but the purification of upayoga.

11. Emotion, passion, and attention

Kundakunda’s philosophy of mind refuses to separate cognition from affect. Passions such as attachment, aversion, pride, anger, greed, and delusion are not merely emotional episodes; they are epistemic distortions. They make consciousness adhere to what is not itself.

This is why Jain ethics is inseparable from philosophy of mind. Violence, possessiveness, deceit, pride, and craving are wrong not merely because they violate rules, but because they are modes of confused consciousness. They thicken karmic bondage and obstruct knowledge. Conversely, nonviolence, restraint, equanimity, and non-attachment purify cognition.

JAINpedia’s summary of Pravacanasāra is useful here: it distinguishes unsuitable consciousness, aśubha-upayoga, which leads to lower rebirths; suitable consciousness, śubha-upayoga, which leads to heavenly rebirths; and pure consciousness, śuddha-upayoga, which leads toward omniscience.

The key point is that moral quality and cognitive quality are not separate. Wrong attention is karmically productive. Pure attention is liberating.

12. Omniscience as the ideal form of mind

For Kundakunda, the fully liberated or omniscient mind is not an enlarged empirical mind. It is consciousness without obstruction. Pravacanasāra describes omniscience, kevalajñāna, as knowledge of infinite objects across the three times, reflected simultaneously in omniscience. It says that once all karmas hindering the knowledge-nature of the soul are destroyed, pristine knowledge appears; this supreme knowledge is steady, not different from the soul, and free from anxiety. It then identifies omniscience with real happiness.

This is a striking philosophy-of-mind claim: perfect cognition and perfect bliss are identical. Suffering arises from obstruction, misidentification, and affective disturbance. Remove these, and the soul’s intrinsic nature appears as infinite knowledge, perception, energy, and bliss.

This does not fit easily into modern categories. It is not empiricism, because sense experience is limited and karmically conditioned. It is not rationalism in the Cartesian sense, because omniscience is not achieved by inference alone. It is not mysticism in the vague sense, because it is embedded in an exact metaphysical and epistemological system. It is a soteriological epistemology: liberation is the perfection of knowing.

13. Personal identity: enduring soul versus empirical ego

Kundakunda’s view of selfhood is strongly anti-Humean and anti-Buddhist, but not naively egoistic. The empirical ego is a mistaken identification with body, karma, and psychophysical states. Yet behind that mistaken ego is a real enduring soul.

Recent scholarship on Jain selfhood emphasizes that Jain philosophers treated diachronic identity as a major problem, especially in response to Buddhist no-self theories. Ana Bajželj notes that consciousness is the inherent nature of living substance and that it grounds unified individual experience; she also explains that moral responsibility requires continuity between the agent who causes karmic consequences and the living being who later experiences them.

This gives Kundakunda a layered theory of identity:

The empirical personality is contingent and karmic.

The body is non-self.

Memory is not the essence of identity.

Psychological traits are not the essence of identity.

The enduring conscious substance, jīva, is the real bearer of continuity.

The ultimate self is not the narrative ego but the conscious substance capable of liberation.

Compared with Western personal identity theory, Kundakunda is closer to soul-theory than to Lockean memory theory or Humean bundle theory. The SEP’s personal identity entry lists “partless immaterial substances — souls” as one historical option and “bundles of perceptions” as the Humean alternative; Kundakunda clearly rejects the bundle option because he requires a persisting conscious agent-enjoyer for karma and liberation.

14. Kundakunda and the mind-body problem

In the Western tradition, the mind-body problem is often framed as the question of how mental states relate to physical states. The SEP describes dualism as the view that mind and body are both real and fundamentally distinct, with Descartes making thought the essence of mind and extension the essence of body.

Kundakunda is dualist, but his dualism is not Cartesian.

Descartes distinguishes thinking substance from extended substance. Kundakunda distinguishes conscious jīva from non-conscious ajīva. Descartes’ problem becomes: how does an immaterial mind causally interact with a mechanical body? Kundakunda’s problem becomes: how does a conscious soul become karmically associated with non-conscious matter while remaining essentially distinct?

The answer is not Cartesian interaction through a special organ. Nor is it occasionalism. Kundakunda’s answer uses Jain causal pluralism: each substance modifies itself according to its own nature, but substances can serve as instrumental conditions for one another. The soul does not become matter; matter does not become consciousness. But karmic matter can obstruct consciousness, and impure conscious dispositions can attract karmic matter.

This is best described as domain-restricted causal dualism. Consciousness and matter are distinct causal domains, but they are coordinated through instrumental causal relations.

15. Contrast with Sāṃkhya

Kundakunda is often compared with Sāṃkhya because both traditions distinguish conscious self from non-conscious matter and treat liberation as disentanglement. The SEP explicitly notes that Kundakunda’s position is close to Sāṃkhya while retaining the Jain specificity of an essentially active self.

The difference is decisive. In classical Sāṃkhya, puruṣa is a passive witness, while prakṛti performs activity. In Kundakunda, the jīva is not passive in every respect. It is active in its own conscious modes. It knows, mis-knows, attends, attaches, detaches, and realizes. Matter is not a single prakṛti but one ajīva category among several. Karma is not just metaphysical nature but subtle matter bound to the soul through passions.

So Kundakunda can be called Sāṃkhya-like only in a limited sense. He shares the disentanglement project, but not Sāṃkhya’s exact metaphysics of passivity and material agency.

16. Contrast with Buddhist no-self

Kundakunda opposes Buddhist no-self at the deepest level. For him, there must be a real enduring conscious substance. Without it, karmic responsibility, rebirth, liberation, and omniscience become unintelligible.

Yet Kundakunda also agrees with Buddhists against naïve egoism. The ordinary “I” that identifies with body, sensations, passions, and possessions is false. The empirical ego is not the true self. In that respect, Kundakunda offers a middle position: he rejects the empirical ego, but not the metaphysical self.

The result is not anātman but purified ātman/jīva. The self that must be abandoned is the karmically deluded ego; the self that must be realized is pure consciousness.

17. Contrast with Vedānta

Kundakunda sometimes sounds Vedāntic because he speaks of the pure self, inner realization, and the distinction between conventional and ultimate standpoints. JAINpedia explicitly says that Kundakunda’s writings can be compared with Vedānta because both stress inner spiritual life.

But the differences are substantial. Kundakunda is not an Advaitin. He does not reduce all selves to one Brahman. He does not treat the world as mere illusion. He preserves Jain pluralism, Jain karma realism, Jain nonviolence, and Jain many-sided ontology. There are infinitely many souls, each individually capable of liberation. The pure self is not the one universal consciousness of Advaita; it is the purified individual jīva.

18. Comparison with Plato and Aristotle

Kundakunda resembles Plato in treating the soul as separable from bodily identity and in regarding liberation as a kind of release from bodily entanglement. But Plato’s framework is shaped by Forms, recollection, and the tripartite soul, while Kundakunda’s is shaped by karma, upayoga, and the distinction between jīva and ajīva. Kundakunda also extends soulhood far beyond the human realm; Jainism attributes souls to many forms of life, including beings with one sense, such as plant and elemental-bodied beings.

Aristotle is much further from Kundakunda. Aristotle’s hylomorphism treats the soul as the form or actuality of a living body and generally gives no reason to think the soul survives apart from the body, although Aristotle’s account of nous is complex. The SEP notes that Aristotle’s soul-body theory embraces neither reductive materialism nor Platonic dualism. Kundakunda, by contrast, is a robust dualist about conscious substance and non-conscious matter.

So, relative to ancient Western philosophy, Kundakunda is closer to Plato than Aristotle on separability, but unlike both because of Jain pluralism, karma matter, and universalized non-human sentience.

19. Comparison with Descartes

Kundakunda and Descartes converge on one central claim: consciousness is not reducible to body. Both distinguish the conscious subject from the material organism. Both make consciousness central to selfhood.

But the differences are more important.

Descartes defines mind as thinking substance and body as extended substance; Kundakunda defines soul as conscious jīva and body, speech, physical mind, and karma as pudgala. Descartes’ central modern problem is interaction between mind and body; Kundakunda’s central problem is karmic bondage between soul and matter. Descartes’ account is strongly human-centered, whereas Jainism grants souls across a vast range of living beings. Descartes’ mind is not spatially coextensive with the body in the Jain sense; the Jain soul, while embodied, occupies the body’s space like light in a room.

Kundakunda also differs from Descartes because he does not make the pure self a willful ego. The liberated soul does not command the world. It knows and sees without attachment, desire, or anxiety.

20. Comparison with Hume and modern bundle theories

Hume famously replaces the substantial self with a bundle of perceptions. The SEP’s personal identity entry lists the Humean “bundle of perceptions” view as one major option.

Kundakunda would regard this as a confusion of the self with its modes. Perceptions, emotions, bodily states, memories, and mental images are paryāyas, modifications, not the substance itself. They change. The jīva persists. In Jain terms, Hume describes the empirical stream but misses the conscious substance whose modifications form that stream.

Yet Kundakunda would agree with Hume that the ordinary ego cannot be found as a stable object among experiences. The difference is that Hume concludes there is no substantial self; Kundakunda concludes that the real self is not an empirical object among other objects but the luminous subject-substance.

21. Comparison with Brentano, phenomenology, and intentionality

Kundakunda’s account of knowledge has obvious relevance to intentionality. Brentano and later philosophers treat intentionality as the mind’s directedness toward objects. Kundakunda also treats consciousness as object-directed: the soul knows objects, and knowledge may reflect all knowables.

But Kundakunda does not reduce consciousness to intentionality. Upayoga includes object-directed cognition, but pure consciousness also has reflexive self-manifestation. In the perfected state, the soul knows itself and all objects without desire, effort, or acquisitive intentionality. Consciousness is not exhausted by representation; representation is one manifestation of consciousness.

Phenomenologically, Kundakunda resembles theories of pre-reflective self-awareness: consciousness reveals itself in the very act of revealing objects. But unlike many modern phenomenologists, he treats this not only as a structure of experience but as a metaphysical fact about the soul.

22. Comparison with physicalism and mind-brain identity theory

Modern physicalism often identifies mental states with brain states or physical processes. The SEP describes the mind-brain identity theory as the view that states and processes of mind are identical to states and processes of the brain, with experiences such as pain or seeing being brain processes rather than merely correlated with them.

Kundakunda rejects this at the root. Consciousness is not a brain process. Indeed, in Jain metaphysics the physical mind is itself a subtle material instrument, not the conscious subject. Brain, body, senses, and material mind can condition cognition, but they cannot generate consciousness because consciousness belongs to jīva alone.

However, Kundakunda is not anti-materialist in a crude sense. He gives matter enormous explanatory scope. Matter explains bodies, karmic particles, sensory instruments, speech, and embodied cognition. His view is dualist about consciousness but quasi-physicalist about the apparatus and obstructions of empirical mentation.

This makes his view more nuanced than simple “soul versus body” dualism. It says: the conscious subject is non-material, but empirical mental life is deeply conditioned by subtle material processes.

23. Comparison with contemporary consciousness debates

Modern philosophy of consciousness asks how consciousness fits into nature. The SEP describes consciousness as perhaps the central issue in current theorizing about mind and notes that any adequate account of mind must explain consciousness and its relation to non-conscious reality.

Kundakunda’s answer is direct: consciousness is not something that emerges from non-conscious matter. It is an irreducible feature of jīva. This avoids the hard problem in one sense by denying the premise that consciousness must be generated by matter. But it inherits a different problem: how exactly do non-conscious karmic particles obstruct, condition, or coordinate with non-material consciousness?

Compared with contemporary qualia debates, Kundakunda would not treat phenomenal character as a mysterious add-on to physical processing. The SEP defines qualia broadly as the introspectively accessible phenomenal aspects of mental life, “what it is like” to undergo experience. Kundakunda would say that experiential manifestation belongs to the soul’s upayoga, while specific pleasure, pain, and affective coloration are karmically conditioned modes.

Compared with panpsychism, Kundakunda is again close but different. Panpsychism treats consciousness as fundamental and widely distributed in the material world; the SEP notes that some panpsychists take consciousness to be part of the intrinsic nature of matter. Kundakunda does not. Consciousness is fundamental, but it is not the intrinsic nature of pudgala. It belongs to jīva, not to matter as matter.

24. Kundakunda’s reconstructed philosophy of mind: systematic theses

A comprehensive Kundakundian philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as the following theses.

First, consciousness is ontologically basic. It is not produced by matter, body, brain, or physical mind. It is the defining mark of jīva.

Second, the subject is not a bare owner of mental states. The subject is intrinsically conscious. From the ultimate standpoint, the soul and knowledge are non-different.

Third, consciousness is reflexive. It illuminates itself and its objects, like a lamp.

Fourth, consciousness is intentional but not reducible to intentionality. It knows objects without becoming them and without objects becoming mental fictions.

Fifth, ordinary mental life is karmically conditioned. Cognitive limitation, affective distortion, bodily embodiment, and moral bondage are aspects of one psychophysical condition.

Sixth, error is self-misidentification. The soul mistakes body, karma, passions, pleasure, pain, and empirical personality for itself.

Seventh, agency is primarily transformation of conscious modes. The soul acts in its own domain; matter acts in its own domain; each can be an instrumental condition for the other.

Eighth, moral psychology and epistemology are inseparable. Passions obscure knowledge. Non-attachment purifies cognition.

Ninth, personal identity is grounded in enduring jīva, not memory, body, narrative, or bundle-like psychological continuity.

Tenth, liberation is the full manifestation of consciousness as omniscience and bliss. The perfected mind is not a better empirical mind; it is consciousness unobstructed.

25. Internal tensions and philosophical problems

Kundakunda’s system is powerful, but it has tensions.

The first is the relation between pure non-agency and practical effort. If the pure soul is already distinct from karma, why is discipline needed? Kundakunda’s answer is perspectival: from niścaya the soul is pure; from vyavahāra the embodied soul must practice restraint, right knowledge, and non-attachment. The danger is quietism if niścaya is absolutized without vyavahāra.

The second is the interaction problem. Kundakunda avoids Cartesian interactionism by using Jain instrumental causality, but a modern philosopher may still ask how non-conscious matter can obstruct consciousness without either becoming conscious or violating the separateness of domains.

The third is individuation after liberation. If liberated souls are all omniscient, passionless, and karmically unobstructed, what differentiates them? Jainism generally preserves plurality even among liberated souls, but the more one emphasizes pure consciousness, the more individuation requires careful metaphysical explanation.

The fourth is the status of empirical psychology. Kundakunda’s ultimate standpoint may seem to devalue ordinary mental life: memory, emotion, personality, imagination, and social identity. His likely response is that these are real conventionally but not ultimately constitutive of the self.

The fifth is textual-historical. Because the Kundakunda corpus is layered and heavily mediated by later commentators such as Amṛtacandra and Jayasena, any “Kundakundian philosophy of mind” is partly a reconstruction of a tradition, not merely the doctrine of one securely datable author.

26. Why Kundakunda matters for the global philosophy of mind

Kundakunda offers a major alternative to the dominant Western map of options.

He is not an Aristotelian hylomorphist, because the conscious self is not merely the form of the body.

He is not a Cartesian dualist, because the soul is not an isolated res cogitans interacting mechanically with extended matter.

He is not a Humean bundle theorist, because the self is an enduring conscious substance.

He is not a physicalist, because consciousness is not identical with brain or body.

He is not a panpsychist, because consciousness belongs to jīva, not to matter as such.

He is not an Advaitin monist, because individual souls remain real and plural.

He is not a Buddhist no-self theorist, because liberation requires the realization of the true conscious self.

His distinctive contribution is a theory in which consciousness is intrinsic, self-luminous, morally modulated, karmically obstructed, and ultimately perfectible. He gives us a philosophy of mind in which the central problem is not merely “How does the brain produce consciousness?” but “How does consciousness mistake the non-self for itself, and how can it recover its unobstructed nature?”

That shift is profound. In Kundakunda, philosophy of mind is not a detached theoretical inquiry. It is a path of cognitive purification. To know what mind is, one must distinguish the knower from what is merely reflected in knowing. To solve the problem of mind is to end misidentification. To perfect mind is to become omniscient, passionless, and free.

PreviousŚāntarakṣita NextUmāsvāti

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
